title: Quantum Magic in early FTQC: From Diagonal Clifford Hierarchy No-Go Theorems to Architecture Design Blueprints
authors: Hsueh-Hao Lu, Yasunari Suzuki, Yasunobu Nakamura, En-Jui Kuo
abstract: We address the circuit-design problem of maximizing quantum magic in early fault-tolerant quantum computing (early FTQC), where logical dynamics natively take the form of alternating Clifford layers and diagonal non-Clifford layers. To render this optimization analytically tractable, we first prove a uniqueness theorem: for operational magic functionals built from Pauli expectation values, the axioms of faithfulness and tensor-product additivity force a Rényi-type dependence on the Pauli-spectrum. Leveraging the closed phase-polynomial description of the diagonal Clifford hierarchy, we derive exact Pauli-spectrum expressions and tight bounds for a shallow-layer model. These bounds expose a zero-magic mechanism and prove that maximal magic strictly requires graph-state preconditioning. Consequently, we establish our first no-go theorem: hierarchy level alone cannot universally order operational magic. Extending our framework to the $N$-layer model motivated by the Space-Time Efficient Analog Rotation (STAR) architecture, we obtain an exact iterative update rule for the Pauli spectrum. This yields a second no-go theorem: no state-independent sequence of operations can guarantee monotonic magic improvement. Together, these theorems demonstrate that algebraic gate structures are fundamentally insufficient to dictate resource generation. To overcome this, we reframe early FTQC gate selection as a state-aware, differentiable optimization over continuous analog parameters. Finally, we identify a severe kinematic expressibility bottleneck in architectures restricted to single-qubit $Z$-rotations and show that introducing nonlinear diagonal phases, such as multi-qubit $Z$-rotation, shatters this bottleneck. This provides a fundamental principle for demonstrating early FTQC, establishing scalable magic generation as a foundational benchmark for evaluating early FTQC architectures.
summary: This paper provides a fundamental framework for maximizing non-stabilizer resources in early fault-tolerant quantum computers. It proves that gate hierarchy and state-independent sequences are insufficient for magic generation, establishing that optimal architecture design must be state-aware and utilize multi-qubit interactions.
main_problem: Optimizing the generation of quantum magic (non-stabilizerness) in early fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC) architectures by selecting optimal gate sequences and input states.
main_result: The authors prove two no-go theorems: the Clifford hierarchy level alone cannot universally order magic, and no state-independent sequence of operations can guarantee monotonic magic improvement. They also identify a bottleneck in single-qubit rotation architectures and propose multi-qubit diagonal phases as a solution.
method: The study uses the resource theory of magic, Pauli-spectrum analysis, and the algebraic properties of the Clifford hierarchy, employing symplectic formalism and phase-polynomial representations to derive analytical bounds.
model_or_system: The paper analyzes early FTQC circuit models, specifically a shallow-layer model (one Clifford layer followed by a diagonal hierarchy gate) and an N-layer model (alternating Clifford and diagonal layers) inspired by the STAR architecture.
key_observables: Quantum magic (non-stabilizerness), Pauli spectrum (expectation values), stabilizer Renyi entropy, and Pauli rank (support size).
important_parameters: Clifford hierarchy level (k), number of pure-Z generators (r), number of layers (N), and phase-polynomial parameters.
main_assumptions: The analysis focuses on pure quantum states and assumes ideal, high-fidelity Clifford operations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the structural classification of the analyzed architectures; Figure 2 compares standard T-doped random circuits with the early FTQC framework; Figure 3 illustrates the organization of magic measures; Figure 4 depicts the shallow-layer and N-layer circuit models.
paper_structure: The paper begins by classifying magic measures and establishing a uniqueness theorem for Pauli-spectrum functionals. It then analyzes the shallow-layer model to derive zero-magic and maximal-magic conditions, followed by an investigation of the N-layer model to present no-go theorems regarding hierarchy and monotonic improvement. Finally, it identifies architectural bottlenecks and proposes multi-qubit rotation strategies.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Structural classification of the quantum circuit ar- chitectures analyzed in this work. The overarching domain represents the capabilities of early fault-tolerant quantum com- puting (FTQC). The shallow-layer model (ansatz 1) utilizes a generalized multi-qubit diagonal gate (D1) following a Clif- ford operation (C1). This model captures the broad algebraic capacity of early FTQC but operates outside the restrictive Space-Time efficient Analog Rotation (STAR) paradigm (blue boundary). In contrast, ansatz 2 explicitly belongs to the STAR subset, utilizing an N-layer alternating sequence of Clifford operations (Ci) and localized single-qubit diagonal Z-rotation. In these commonly analyzed models, non-stabilizerness is analytically evaluated by averaging over ensembles of uniformly distributed random Clifford blocks Ci ∼Uniform(C(2) n ) to form unitary 3-designs that approximate Haar-random states [23, 25, 28]. However, this framework relies on the perfect gate accuracy of mature fault-tolerant hardware. (b) A deterministic early FTQC framework analyzed this work. FIG. 5. Schematic diagram of the nontrivial group overlap, excluding the identity element. The red region denotes the simultaneous overlap between two computational-basis states and the stabilizer state, which determines the nonzero contri- butions to the Pauli spectrum.
